engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: The centre break came a move before it was ready
mistakes.explanation: 4…e5 is the right idea in the wrong move order. After 5.dxe5 there is no good way to get the pawn back: 5…Nxe5?? just loses to 6.Nxe5, because only the c6-knight covered e5, and the standard fork trick 5…d4 bounces off 6.Bxd4! — the odd-looking 2.Be3 had quietly covered d4 all along. Your 5…Ne4 was the best try, but 6.Qxd5! would have kept both pawns (6…Bf5 7.Qc4 Nxc3 8.bxc3), and even after Maia's 6.Nxe4 dxe4, 7.Qxd8+ Nxd8 8.Ng5! rounds up e4 while e5 stands. Maia played 7.Nd4? instead and 7…Nxe5 restored the balance — the pawn came back as a gift, not by right. Simply developing with 4…Bf5 keeps a comfortable, equal position where …e5 can come later with support.

takeaways.lesson: Before a pawn break, count attackers and defenders on the arrival square.
takeaways.detail: e5 was hit twice — by the d4-pawn and the f3-knight — and defended once, by the c6-knight. When the count comes up short, the break only works if a concrete tactic regains the pawn; calculate that line to the end before pushing, or play a developing move instead.

takeaways.lesson: Ask what your opponent's odd-looking move defends before relying on a standard trick.
takeaways.detail: 2.Be3 looked passive, but it covered d4 — exactly the square the usual …e5-and-…d4 fork needs. When an opponent plays an offbeat move, find the one routine idea it takes away from you, and re-check your plans against it.

mistakes.title: Mate in one, promoted two moves late
mistakes.explanation: The fastest finish was sitting on b2: 50…b1=Q#. The new queen checks the king on a2 from next door, your queen on c1 sweeps a3 through the square the pawn just vacated, and the bishop on c3 seals b2 behind it — every escape square covered, in a single move. 50…Qa1+ won too, but it let the king slide out to b3 and the hunt ran on to move 56. This was the story of the whole final phase: from move 46 the engine found a faster mate on nearly every turn — 49…Qc1+ was mate in two, and on move 55, with two queens on the board, 55…Qb2# was again mate on the spot while 55…Qed4 gave the king one more move to breathe. Two queens against a bare king should be a two-move job.

takeaways.lesson: When the enemy king is bare, check every check — including promotions — before playing a routine one.
takeaways.detail: Promotion mates are the easiest to miss because the mating piece isn't on the board yet. In any winning position, make the last scan before you move: list every check you have, imagine each one played, and ask if the king has a reply. Do it especially on the move you promote.

takeaways.lesson: Drill the basic queen mates until the finish is automatic.
takeaways.detail: Set up king-and-queen versus king and two-queens versus king against a computer and practice mating in the minimum number of moves: use one queen to box the king to the edge a rank at a time, then deliver mate with the second piece. Five minutes of this a week removes the move-50-to-56 wander for good.

mistakes.title: The king had an escort job — you sent the g-pawn instead
mistakes.explanation: After 28.c4 White's entire plan is one pawn wide: push c5 and pray. But your bishop on e3 already covered c5, so the counterplay was never going through — the position was a race with only one runner in it. The fast plan is 28…Kg5!: the king walks g5–g4–f3 to escort your e-pawn home while the bishop keeps an eye on the queenside pawns (the engine's line runs 29.Kc2 Kg4 30.c5 Bf4! 31.Kd1 Kf3, and the e-pawn is unstoppable). 28…g6 set the slowest pawn on the board in motion instead. You still ate c5, b4 and a3 when they came, and the g-pawn did eventually queen — but the win took until move 56 instead of the mid-forties.

takeaways.lesson: In a won endgame, pick your fastest passed pawn and escort it with the king.
takeaways.detail: Count the moves to promotion for each candidate pawn, king steps included, and commit to the shortest count. Here the e-pawn with the king coming to f3 beats the g-pawn by many moves — the comparison takes ten seconds and saves ten moves.

takeaways.lesson: Before reacting to counterplay, check whether a piece already stops it.
takeaways.detail: The e3-bishop covered c5, so White's only pawn break was already dead. When you spot the opponent's plan, first ask which of your pieces deals with it as they stand — a threat that's already answered costs you zero tempi.

mistakes.title: One recapture walked into castling-with-check
mistakes.explanation: Both recaptures on d8 win the queen back, but they are not equal. 11…Rxd8 keeps the king on e8 and plants the rook on the open file first — if White castles long anyway, it's just a rook trade on your terms. After 11…Kxd8, White got the rare treat of 12.O-O-O+!: one move that develops a rook, attacks your king and wins a tempo all at once. Your king had to walk to e7, White owned the d-file for free, and 16.Rd1+ hit the king again a few moves later. Up a full piece it changed nothing — but every free tempo handed to a worse position is an invitation, and the same habit in a close game costs real points.

takeaways.lesson: When two recaptures work, take with the piece that doesn't expose your king to the next check.
takeaways.detail: Before recapturing, run one quick scan per option: what checks does my opponent have against each? Here 12.O-O-O+ existed against the king recapture and simply didn't exist against 11…Rxd8 — a ten-second comparison.

takeaways.lesson: Treat castling as a real move in your threat scan — it can arrive with check.
takeaways.detail: Long castling puts a rook on d1 in one move, and it's easy to forget because the rook starts far from the action. Whenever the enemy king is still home and the d- or f-file is opening, add O-O-O and O-O to the list of replies you check.
